title: CLAUDE.md
text: socialchange R package decomposing social change intraindividual change vs. population turnover. separates aggregate-level change time : - Intraindividual/within-unit change (people changing attitudes) - Population turnover (cohort replacement, mortality, migration, coming--age) - Event-driven change (countries joining/leaving EU) Intellectual positioning: (1) critique APC methods — suffer identification problem (age = period − cohort) need untestable assumptions; package offers directly interpretable decomposition alternatives. (2) improvement Firebaugh’s algebraic/linear CR-IC decomposition (mishandles entering/exiting cohorts, non-linearities, leaves residuals) via model-based counterfactuals (cr_ic()) simulation-based individual-level decomposition (sim_social_change(), decompose_aggregated()) tracks demographic events directly.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Three main decomposition approaches, using data.table, formula interfaces (Outcome ~ Unit + Time), S3 classes custom print()/plot(): Event-based (R/decompose_events.R) — decompose_events(): units enter/exit discrete times. Two datasets (events + outcomes). Separates “change” (within-unit) “replacement” (turnover) counterfactuals. → class decompose_events Aggregated (R/decompose_aggregated.R) — decompose_aggregated(): data aggregated age + covariates. Microsimulation randomly orders demographic events. Needs stacked panel data (age, period, cell counts) fitted model (lm/glm/gam) predicting outcome age/period/cells. Decomposes intraindividual change, coming--age, mortality, net -migration. Optional population arg supplies true cell × period count frame (overrides survey counts). R > 0 adds model-uncertainty SEs via Dirichlet bootstrap. → class social_change_decomp Simulation (R/simulate.R) — sim_social_change(): forward simulation. User supplies functions outcome (fun_y), mortality, coming--age, migration, state transitions. Returns event--event records. → class social_change_sim Additional methods: - CR-IC (R/cr_ic.R) — classical cohort-replacement vs. intraindividual change (algebraic, linear, model-based). → class cr_ic_decomposition - APC (R/apc.R) — APC models via orthogonal polynomial contrasts; includes plot_gam_surface(). → class apc_model Functions rename input columns standardized names internally; period columns coerced numeric.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Events derived cell-count differences (“never residual”): survivors mortality = pmax(0, n1 - n2), inmigration = pmax(0, n2 - n1); new cohorts coming_of_age = pmax(0, n2 - n1). Consequences: Migration: net -migration, residual. -migration never separately identified (survivor loss death exit, ’s folded mortality; reported outmigration always 0). raw survey cells -migration term mostly sampling noise (~1.9% GSS homosexuality example); meaningful true population frame. Migration among coming--age cohorts modeled. Top-coding → mortality. survivor cohort aging past observed age range booked deaths (GSS top-codes age 89; 18–65 sample sends everyone aging past 65 mortality). Design choice, bug — n1 - n2 accounting. practice: use open-ended top age, read mortality “deaths + exits past window top”. Transitions misattributed. Within-cell transitions (e.g. smokers → non-smokers) separately identified get absorbed intraindividual change; pmax(0, …) guard masks offsetting flows (cell net transition inflow shows 0 mortality even deaths occurred). Use data without significant transitions. sim_social_change() tracks transitions properly can’t recover aggregated data. Survey weights approximation. individual-level weight, weights normalized within period sum .N, n = round(sum(normalized_weight)). Adjusts relative structure correctly keeps event counts tractable, doesn’t recover absolute population sizes; rounding adds small errors. Prefer supplying true population counts available.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Profiled GSS homosexuality (34,026 rows, 26 periods, ages 21–89). GAM fun_y (s(age)+s(period)) ~75% wall time user’s model prediction, simulation logic: GAM call ~1.43 s (~1.08 s predict.gam/PredictMat), lm call ~0.35 s; simulation machinery ~0.35 s. simulate_schedule() evaluates fun_y n_cells × (n_ev+1) stacked frame, cost scales n_cells × n_ev × cost(fun_y) — bootstrap multiplies R. Remaining leverage: O(n_ev × n_cells × R) replay loop replay_schedule(). R-level wins (lpmatrix basis reuse across replicates, draw-vectorized replay) spent; deterministic (-RNG) replay natural unit C++ port. period loop embarrassingly parallel wrong axis (breaks RNG reproducibility, lowest leverage); bootstrap, parallelize R draws instead.

text: Remaining: demographic-uncertainty branch (), rest (b). Architecture keeps three inputs decoupled pluggable event generator : Reference (untested, may bugs): old_scripts/functions/functions_clean.R — eventDecompositionMat, fitBSModels/splineForBS, createPopFrame/calcSurvivingPop, prepComponentData/decomposeChangeByVar. Feature (): Standard errors. Three uncertainty sources: 1. Model uncertainty — implemented. R > 0 refits model R Dirichlet-reweighted copies training data (y_replicates() generic R/y_model.R), replaying event schedule (common random numbers). Returns long per-(draw, period, cell) draws table; print/plot derive cumulative 95% CI (cumulative_series()). Reweighting microdata (vcov() posterior sim) deliberate — captures misspecification survey-weight variance. 2. Simulation uncertainty — Monte Carlo noise random ordering; averaged via common random numbers, reported. 3. Demographic uncertainty — unimplemented. Sampling error n1/n2 supplied mortality/migration. population, bootstrap reweights survey, external counts carry uncertainty. Deferred limitations bootstrap: survey design ignored (reweights rows PSUs); refit reads original data name (getCall()$data), breaks scope; refitting transiently holds R refits (predictor keeps coefficients); replicates reuse one design matrix, model whose prediction isn’t fixed linkinv(Xβ) (weight-dependent basis) errors rather falling back per-replicate prediction. Feature (b): Migration estimation. Per cell identity n2 = n1 + coming_of_age + inmigration − deaths − outmigration. survivors ’s 1 equation, 3 unknowns — net migration identified. User closes system inputs supply: - Default (current): net change → coming--age / mortality (shrinking) / net -migration (growing); -migration = 0. - Supply mortality (recommended): deaths = fun_mortality(cell), net_migration = n2 − (n1 − deaths); positive ⇒ , negative ⇒ . - Supply net migration → back deaths. - Supply → -determined; reconcile / warn beyond tolerance. Proposed API: fun_mortality = NULL, fun_migration = NULL, consumed derive_events(). Default NULL = current pmax() strategy. Print/plot already render migration rows non-zero; supplying inputs makes -migration appear. Modeling choices document: () migrants take receiving cell’s mean Y; (ii) migration among coming--age cohorts ignored; (iii) net-. Overlap & sequencing: (b) defines event table; ()’s demographic branch perturbs . converge derive_events(). (b)’s remaining half first, ()’s bootstrap pluggable inputs. Possible future helper — raking. adds value marginal population targets joint distribution (can’t supply population frame). IPF reconstructs adjusted joint n preprocessing (survey::rake/anesrake/autumn); sketch harmonize_cells(data, targets, , within="period"). value-add enforcing: rake within wave, re-rake inside bootstrap draw SE wrong. near-term.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: now feed simulated data event-based decomposition: event-based decomposition exactly recovered results.
code: library("data.table")  data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39),     n = c(rep(100, 10), seq.int(100, 10, by = -10)) )  support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, (age - 20) * 1 / 20] } coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = 20, n = 100) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 10, 0)] }  library("socialchange") simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial                0.3758 #>  At end                    0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Out-migration         0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #>    - In-migration          0.0000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) # we know that y depends only on age here model <- lm(y ~ age, data = stacked_data)  decompresult <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, model) print(decompresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)      0.3758         #>  At end (modeled)          0.3758         #>  Total change              0.0000         #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500         #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500         #>    - Mortality            -0.1208         #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1292

code: coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90 - period * 15, 10 + period * 15)) }  smoking3 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking3, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.86769 #>  At end                    0.50476 #>  Total change             -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29126 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07167 #>    - Mortality            -0.01073 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06094 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking3$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period * smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, model, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)      0.86769         #>  At end (modeled)          0.50476         #>  Total change             -0.36293   100.0 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29131   80.3  #>  - Population turnover    -0.07161   19.7  #>    - Mortality            -0.01077   3.0   #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06084   16.8

text: homosex outcome rescaled [0, 1], 0 means “always wrong” 1 means “wrong ”. table gives summary statistics; line plot shows clear upward trend acceptance survey period.  separate period cohort effects, fit GAM two-dimensional smooth survey year birth cohort. 3D surface contour plot shows acceptance increased strongly among younger cohorts later periods.
code: # compare to Table 1 in Ekstam -- roughly similar modelsummary::datasummary(   homosex + year + cohort + age + educ ~ mean + SD + min + max,   data = gss_homosex, output = "markdown", fmt = 3) # sex and race are categorical, so show their level breakdown instead modelsummary::datasummary(   sex + race ~ N + Percent(),   data = gss_homosex, output = "markdown", fmt = 1) by_year <- gss_homosex[, list(y = weighted.mean(homosex, wtssall)), by = c("year")] ggplot(by_year, aes(x = year, y = y)) + geom_line() + ylim(0, 1) + theme_light() splinemodel = gam(homosex ~ s(year, cohort), data = gss_homosex) vis.gam(splinemodel, theta = 40) plot_gam_surface(splinemodel)

title: Decomposing General Social Survey data: Attitudes on Homosexuality
text: cr_ic() function implements four methods separating intracohort change (IC) cohort replacement (CR): algebraic decomposition (AD), linear decomposition (LD), two model-based improvements (AD+ Model). detailed explanation method, including replication Firebaugh (1989), see Replications: Firebaugh vignette. Acceptance rose 43 percentage points 1973 2018 (0.19 0.62). Using full panel, two model-based methods attribute larger share intracohort change, though differ exact split: AD+ gives roughly 63% intracohort change 37% cohort replacement, Model method gives roughly 56% 44%.  first last survey years used, balance shifts slightly toward cohort replacement (~54% CR, ~46% IC). illustrates choice time points can affect decomposition results, model-based AD+ Model methods preferred simpler AD.

code: # decompose_aggregated() requires every wave to share a common minimum age. The # 2014 and 2016 waves sampled no one under 19 and 21 respectively, so we restrict # to age >= 21 to give all waves the same minimum age. gss_all <- gss_homosex[age >= 21, .(age, period = year, sex, y = homosex, wtssall)] set.seed(42) model <- mgcv::gam(y ~ s(age) + s(period), data = gss_all, weights = wtssall) result <- decompose_aggregated(gss_all, model, weight = "wtssall") print(result, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)      0.19739         #>  At end (modeled)          0.56751         #>  Total change              0.37013   100.0 #>  - title: Simulating social change
text: Building simulation requires three steps: Define population Define outcome function variable interest Define population dynamics (coming age, mortality, - -migration) Step 1: first step define initial population. example, define simple population aged 20 39, uniform distribution age 30, declining age 39. dataset define needs least two columns: age n. can add columns want describe complicated populations, ’ll stick simple setup now. ’ll call row dataset population cell. extreme case, can also define population individuals n just 1. ’s simple population: Step 2: Next, define variable interest want simulate. , define function takes arguments population cells time continuous variable. Time really needs continuous - need know outcome every individual population terms discrete ‘years’ (whatever time scale), every point -well. example, use outcome dependent age: age 20, outcome 0, age 40 outcome 1, values linearly increasing age. purely function age, time, real-world survey, outcome “support investing longevity research” “support increasing pensions” - ’ll just use latter example: defined population terms one variable (age), members age also identical outcome value. Step 3: next step define population dynamics. requires defining four functions: fun_mortality(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_outmigration(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_coming_of_age(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n fun_inmigration(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n functions defined period--period. practice, often years. instance, mortality function return counts define many members population die within next period, population cell. -migration function defined way, just number people migrating population cell. coming age function -migration functions different: return data frame describes new population members added period--period. functions flexible enough describe complicated real-world population dynamics. first simple example, define functions way keeps population stable: don’t migration, mortality coming--age balances perfectly - every period 100 new members age 20 added, 100 members age 30 die. four functions optional, just define coming--age mortality function: ’re now ready start simulation: simulation result displays overview period, well summary total decomposition change. results year show indeed population stable: end every period population 1550, stable mean 0.38 - .e, 38% population supports increasing pensions. However, stability bit deceptive, everyone’s opinion actually keeps constantly changing. can see looking “Intraindividual change” component, +0.25. Roughly speaking, without population turnover, expected mean support increasing pensions increased quite bit, 63%. makes sense, ’ve designed simulation way support increases age. turn, younger people much less likely support increasing pensions. Hence, enter population, effect decreasing mean. true mortality. Hence, population turnover component negative. simulation, two components balance exactly, leading expected complete stabilty year--year mean. slightly complex setup, can add covariates simulation. instance, can introduce gender covariate. example, split former population two equally-distributed genders: also adjust population dynamics functions keep population stable. Note don’t depend gender (): reran simulation settings, get exact results . introduce slight variation, though, determine one gender actually fixed opinion support increasing pensions. gender behaves : Although overall mean doesn’t change time, decomposition results changed. men now contribute population turnover intraindividual change (women ), components half . previous scenario, overall outcome stable, intraindividual change well population turnover, cancelling . can also construct case shows intraindividual change, making outcome function dependent time (possibly covariates, ), age. expected, results show outcome increased 0.1 year--year, change due intraindividual change: Conversely, can also construct scenario change explained population turnover - older cohorts replaced younger cohorts. , define outcome function individual’s birth cohort: constants chosen two cases align. important thing outcome depends birth cohort (.e. cohort = year - age). results show exact dynamic – initial mean 0.3 increase 0.1 every year –, different explanations: Now change completely explained population turnover. one ever changes mind, social change occurs older cohorts low outcome values die younger cohorts higher outcome values follow. Hence, mortality coming--age contribute equally total change.

text: second example, try simulate somewhat realistic scenario attitudes towards smoking changed Western societies. start simple build complex simulations piece piece. outcome, consider survey question “smoking public allowed?”. start similar population , time split smokers non-smokers, vast majority population smokes (90% younger age groups). construct initial population already way shows differential mortality: Smokers die early, meaning higher ages, proportion non-smokers increases: disregard migration, create coming--age mortality function reproduces current population: Lastly, define static outcome differs smokers non-smokers, otherwise stable: 90% smokers agree smoking public allowed, 60% non-smokers : Running simulation scenario shows static population: neither intra-individual change net effect population turnover, although mortality decreases mean (smokers die earlier, drawing mean), coming--age brings back . two forces therefore perfectly cancel . slightly realistic scenario, assume smokers non-smokers change mind smoking public, however, smokers change mind half rate compared non-smokers: expected, population turnover component still effectively zero, showing dynamics . However, now substantial intraindividual change, means outcome decreases 87% 59% - due intraindividual change. attitudes towards smoking changing, likely also reflected individual behavior. means fewer people may pick smoking first place. can model altering coming--age function: ’re still adding 100 members every year population, every year proportion smokers declines: three quarters first year 15% last year. Given now non-smokers coming age, see now bigger contribution population turnover component, driven coming--age. final mechanism change behavior existing smokers: every year, give smoking, likely also influence opinion smoking public. simplicity, assume former smokers start behave exactly non-smokers moment stop smoking. also keep transitions logic quite simple - assume random set 15% smokers become non-smokers every period. make complicated, instance, also stratifying transitions age. now include transition logic simulation: now see bigger contribution intraindividual change people become nonsmokers start share group’s opinion smoking public. side effect new logic population longer stable: number people population increasing, mean age. now also people grow older 39, mortality function setup didn’t account people attaining older ages: side effect fewer people smoking, decreases chance mortality. complicated simulation might introduce “former smoker” group retains mortality disadvantage smokers old age. what: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
title: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
text: socialchange package provides methods decomposing aggregate-level social change components intraindividual change population turnover. Population turnover includes replacement individuals (mortality coming--age) migration. package implements event-based, aggregated-data, simulation-based decomposition approaches, well classical cohort replacement vs. intraindividual change (CR-IC) decompositions Age-Period-Cohort (APC) models.

title: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover

what: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: Decomposes total change outcome two periods cohort replacement (CR) intracohort change (IC) components. Four methods available:

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
code: cr_ic(data, formula, weight = NULL, model = NULL)

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: data data frame one row per respondent (one row per cohort-period cell `weight` supplied). formula formula form `outcome ~ period + cohort`. weight Name weight variable string. `NULL`, observations weighted equally. model Either model formula (e.g., `~ period * cohort`) fitted model object supports `predict()`. formula supplied fitted weighted `lm`. Required AD+ Model methods.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: list class `cr_ic_decomposition` components: `summary` `data.table` summarising IC CR full period       method. `detailed` `data.table` year--year results       consecutive pair periods. `periods` Numeric vector periods found data. `cohort` `data.table` cohort-level IC CR contributions       (`model` supplied, otherwise `NULL`). `model` fitted supplied model object, `NULL`.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: - **AD** (algebraic decomposition): Decomposes spanning cohorts IC   CR exactly. Entering exiting cohorts split assigned   entirely CR, Firebaugh notes "problematic". - **LD** (linear decomposition): Fits linear model `y ~ period + cohort`   uses coefficients attribute change. Handles entering/exiting   cohorts capture non-linearities produces residual. - **AD+**: Extends AD imputing missing outcome values entering/exiting   cohorts model supplied via `model`, applies algebraic   decomposition. Produces residual; quality depends model fit. - **Model**: Like AD+ replaces ** cohort-specific values (just   missing ones) model predictions. Acts form regularization,   can help small cohorts noisy estimates. AD LD always computed. AD+ Model require `model` argument.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: Firebaugh, G. (1992). social change come ? Estimating relative contributions intra-cohort change population turnover. *Population Research Policy Review*, 11(1), 61–74.

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: Decomposes aggregate-level change intraindividual change population turnover components using microsimulation stacked cross-sectional data. Requires fitted model predicts outcome function age, period, covariates.

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
code: decompose_aggregated(   stacked_data,   model,   cells = c(),   R = 0,   tol = 0.05,   weight = NULL,   population = NULL,   seed = NULL )  # S3 method for class 'social_change_decomp' plot(x, covariate = NULL, ...)

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: stacked_data Data frame columns age, period, y, plus optional cell identifiers. column n present data treated already aggregated cells; otherwise individual-level rows aggregated internally using weight. model fitted model object (lm, glm, gam) predicting outcome age, period, cells. Predictions taken response scale via predict(). cells Character vector additional cell identifier columns beyond age (e.g., "gender", "smoking") R Number Dirichlet-bootstrap replicates used attach standard errors capturing model uncertainty (default 0, point estimate ). R > 0, model refit R times Dirichlet-reweighted copies training data; spread resulting decompositions (common random numbers, event-ordering noise differenced ) gives per-component standard errors cumulative confidence bands. captures model uncertainty – dominant source – demographic uncertainty cell counts. gam models replicate full refit plus prediction, large R can slow. tol Maximum tolerated absolute deviation observed modeled period means, outcome's units (default 0.05). Checks model reproduces observed period means; largest deviation exceeds tol, function errors. default suits outcomes roughly unit scale (e.g. proportions [0, 1]); set tol match outcomes another scale. weight Name weight column used aggregating individual-level data (ignored n present). Weights normalized within period sum period sample size aggregation, cell counts n (rounded sums normalized weights) reflect relative population structure rather raw sample sizes. preserves simulation tractability approximation: ideal approach use true population counts, generally unavailable survey data alone. population Optional data frame true cell counts n per cell period (columns period, age, cells identifiers, n). supplied, counts replace survey-derived cell counts population frame: drive event derivation weight modeled mean, model continues supply every cell's outcome stacked_data used observed-mean / model-fit diagnostic. preferred input true population counts (e.g. census official statistics) available alongside survey data, sidesteps survey age-structure noise. n rounded whole counts microsimulation, rescale large frames (e.g. raw population counts millions) tractable per-period total first – relative cell structure matters. frame must match survey's minimum age level set cells column, cover every survey period (extra periods dropped); compared rows n > 0. seed Optional integer seed reproducible bootstrap replicates (default NULL). Dirichlet draw always isolated global RNG stream, passing R > 0 never changes point estimate relative R = 0 outer seed. x `social_change_decomp` object returned [decompose_aggregated()]. covariate Optional name single cell covariate (one `cells`, `"age"`) split cumulative change lines. elaborate breakdowns, aggregate `x$record` directly. ... used.

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: S3 object class social_change_decomp components: summary: data.table decomposition components period (including       inmigration outmigration columns; print/plot show migration component       whichever non-zero). record: list per-transition change tables (one per period transition).       table tidy, columns component, cell covariates (age       cells), delta – one row per component per cell, holding cell's total       contribution change component transition. Summed cells       reproduces per-component totals summary. draws: R > 0, long data.table per-(draw, period, cell)       component deltas (columns draw, period, component, delta,       cell covariates) aggregate's confidence band can computed; NULL       R = 0.

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: function estimates mortality, coming--age, net -migration period--period population differences within cells, uses microsimulation randomly order demographic events track contribution aggregate change. Unequal multi-year gaps periods supported: gap exceeds one year, entering cohort assigned specific calendar year within gap crosses minimum age, post-entry aging correctly attributed intraindividual change rather coming--age. waves must share common minimum age (youngest age observed non-zero count); single threshold separates entering cohorts survivors, mismatch across periods error. default survey supplies cell counts outcomes. Supplying population decouples : population frame supplies cell counts n (hence inferred demographic events), model supplies outcomes. reported modeled_mean weighted population frame, whereas observed_mean remains survey's observed mean, two lines may diverge survey population age structures differ. Within cell, survivor cohort shrinks periods loses people mortality, one grows gains people net -migration. net migration recovered: gross -migration separable deaths (survivor loss either), folded mortality reported -migration always zero. cell's net change attributed single event type sign: shrinking cell records mortality (concurrent -migration invisible) growing cell records net -migration (concurrent deaths folded ), offsetting flows within cell seen. New cohorts (minimum age) attribute growth coming--age; migration among entering cohorts modeled. noisy survey cells strategy relabels sampling fluctuation -migration mortality, inferred -migration meaningful population supplies true population frame, growing cohorts reflect genuine net immigration rather survey noise. Limitation: properly handle within-cell state transitions. Transition effects absorbed intraindividual change component.

what: GSS racial attitudes of white Americans — gss_rac
title: GSS racial attitudes of white Americans — gss_rac
text: subset General Social Survey (GSS) containing white respondents valid responses racial attitude questions, covering survey years 1972, 1976, 1980, 1984. outcome variable rac composite scale built four items (RACDIN, RACSEG, RACPUSH, RACMAR), negated z-scored higher values indicate tolerant attitudes. two missing items per respondent imputed rescaling available items. Prepared gssr package.

title: GSS racial attitudes of white Americans — gss_rac
code: gss_rac

title: GSS racial attitudes of white Americans — gss_rac
text: data.table 7 variables: rac Composite racial tolerance scale (higher = tolerant).     Built four z-scored items (negated), shifted +6     give positive values. year GSS survey year (1972, 1976, 1980, 1984). age Age respondent time interview. cohort Birth year, computed year - age. region Region interview (1–9, Census divisions). sex Sex respondent (1 = Male, 2 = Female). wtssall Survey weight.

what: US population by age and sex, 1973–2016 — wpp_us
title: US population by age and sex, 1973–2016 — wpp_us
text: United States population single year age sex, UN World Population Prospects (WPP) 2022, every year 1973 2016 (span gss_homosex survey waves). Ages run 21 89, 89 representing "89 older" match GSS age top-code. Counts thousands reflect true US age/sex structure; intended use population frame decompose_aggregated, relative cell structure matters (rescale per period needed). Prepared via tidywpp package.

title: socialchange 0.1.0
text: Initial release. decompose_events(): event-based decomposition discrete entry/exit populations. decompose_aggregated(): microsimulation-based decomposition aggregated cross-sectional data. sim_social_change(): forward simulation social change full event--event records. cr_ic(): cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decompositions (AD, LD, AD+, Model). apc(): Age-Period-Cohort models using orthogonal polynomial contrasts.
